g.data-narration: Welcome to Basic Propositional Logic - the foundation of logical reasoning in computer science.
A proposition is a declarative statement that is either TRUE or FALSE - never both, never neither.
g.text: A declarative statement that is either TRUE or FALSE

g.data-narration: Six logical connectives form the building blocks: NOT, AND, OR, IF-THEN, IFF, and XOR.
NOT simply flips the truth value: TRUE becomes FALSE, and FALSE becomes TRUE.
AND (conjunction) returns TRUE only when BOTH p and q are TRUE - the strictest operation.
g.text: Only TRUE when BOTH are TRUE

g.data-narration: OR (disjunction) returns TRUE when at least ONE operand is TRUE - inclusive OR.
g.text: TRUE when at least ONE is TRUE

g.data-narration: Implication p → q is FALSE only when p is TRUE and q is FALSE. This is the trickiest connective!
Biconditional p ↔ q is TRUE when both have the SAME truth value - both true or both false.
Remember operator precedence: NOT highest, then AND, OR, Implication, and lowest is Biconditional.
Three special forms: Tautology (always TRUE), Contradiction (always FALSE), Contingency (varies).
Given p → q, learn these transforms: Converse flips, inverse negates both, contrapositive does both.
Key exam formula: p → q is equivalent to ¬p ∨ q. This transformation is called material implication.
g.text: Use this to simplify implication problems!

g.data-narration: Four essential exam tips: Implication is false only T→F, use material implication, contrapositive equals original, converse is not equivalent.
We covered propositions, all six connectives with truth tables, precedence, special forms, and transformations. Practice makes perfect!
Congratulations! You've completed the basic propositional logic module. Test your knowledge with practice problems!
